sentences.text: This is I did-- after my husband passed away, having two babies to look after, that kept me pretty busy.
And having also lots and lots of financial problems, it was terribly hard.
I had very good friends.
I had loyal friends.
I had a couple called Feingold, Jack Feingold and Frieda Feingold, who were extremely nice.
I lived in the country-- and supportive, who even defected their phone regularly and went to see me from time to time, was a great comfort.
I also like to mention here that I had a lady who came in to help me when my husband was alive.
And my husband liked her very much.
And she liked my husband very much, because we lived a very long way from where she lived.
And she had to walk at least two miles to come to us, but she would always do it.
And when I was pregnant with Irene, I nearly lost her at six months.
And I was told to take it easy.
And we asked-- Mrs.
Germany, what she was called--
Yes.
Germany, they called her.
You know, yes, she had lost her son in the war.
In the first few days-- few weeks of the war, she lost her only son.
And she didn't need to go to work, but she felt she needed to get out.
So she took a job with us.
And so she came with us on holiday.
And-- because I was not well, and my husband had a marvelous holiday because I couldn't do much.
Sandra was a baby, and she had somebody to look after her.
And he said it was the best holiday he ever had.
On the journey back from Torquay to London, he was all right.
It is the next day that he-- it was a very hot summer-- he asked to be brought down some beer in the factory, because he was the director of a factory.
And so we took-- she took over some beer for him.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] during the night, he was taken ill.
And he was sick.
And we thought it was the beer.
And he wouldn't let me ring the doctor.
But we did ring the doctor the next day.
And he came, and he said, I think you might have something in your esophagus or something.
Anyway, I don't want to go too long into this, but it took months to diagnose what he had.
And it was hepatitis.
He might have caught it on an office journeys.
But Mrs.
Germany was all the time with us.
When I came to London to have Irene, he died.
And she was six weeks old.
And I stayed another few months.
Conditions were just-- not because of money, because of the attitude of the family was so horrible that I said it was another experience, like a camp experience.
It was terrible.
They didn't understand, had no understanding for what I had gone through and what it meant to me.
Yes.
That's why I went back to Rochester where I lived.
And that Mrs.
Germany came to see me.
And she said, Mrs.
Wineman, I had a dream last night.
And your husband asked me to look after you.
And she did.
It could have been-- and she was a woman in the 50s, late 50s.
It could have been snowing, ice, any weather, she came and she helped me out.
And even when I moved to London, she still came once a month to give me a break.
And she died at the age of 93.
But I can assure you, she was a greatest support, a fantastic person.
She was like their grandmother, and she was marvelous.
So that was-- she helped me a lot.
And when I came to London, the children went to-- Sandra, at least, they were still little, two years old and four years old.
So the-- Sandra went to kindergarten.
And I had a problem with Irene, because Irene was born after he-- when he died.
She was six weeks old.
And I think the family thought she brought bad luck.
And I felt sorry for her because she had been born at such a tragic time.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And probably-- she had little problems, and it was very hard for me to cope with it.
And she probably-- because she was born at such a moment, I probably had even more pity, because really nobody-- Sandra was, when she was born, the princess.
The one that couldn't-- nobody had a child like her.
Father of a first child, he thought she was marvelous, she was wonderful.
Everybody made a fuss of her.
And Irene was born just the other way around.
So I probably gave her a little bit-- I gave her quite a lot of affection.
And therefore, we could never be-- she would never leave me.
So I really needed help.
And I had to send her to kindergarten earlier.
At first, she was against it and she revolted.
But after three months-- in fact, the teacher of that time became my best friend.
After that, she wouldn't miss school.
You know, she loved school.
But she had separation problems.
And today, she's a psychoanalyst herself, so she graduated.
But I really brought them up.

I made up my mind at that time that, really, what I want out of life is to bring those two babes up, not to have a grudge against the world, not to have a thing on our shoulders, you know, to be normal, as normal as possible.
And I-- I said, I will do what I can for them.
I will be there for them.
It was-- that was a kind of task I took on, you know, and a task I wanted to fulfill.
They should be normal human beings.
With all the tragedy that has happened so far, they shall be just normal human beings.
They should be educated, finish their studies, so that they can stand on their two feet and not be like me-- in a foreign country without finished their studies and no support.
There can be nothing worse.
So I struggled on for years and years, till they got through A-levels, and university, and so on and so forth, till I saw their marriage.
And then I felt, now I can relax, you know.
But that was a big, big struggle.
And a hard struggle.
And is that one of the reasons why you didn't remarry?
That's one of the bigger reasons, yes.
And I also was always afraid that if I bring somebody else in, he might not be kind, you know.
People sometimes tried, you know, I should marry somebody.
And usually it was somebody that also had lost a wife and so on, with children.
I couldn't see how we-- I was, what I would call, in a mental-- not in a mental and physical state to take on more.
Do you understand?
Today, probably I say, probably another person would have married again.
Do you understand?
But I was lonely.
I had nobody really to give me a helping hand in that, in everything, to say, well, you know-- what I call a helping hand.
If it happens to somebody to lose a partner, a husband, they're always had a family or they're well-established in this country, or whatever.
They find a way, you know.
I was offered in France, but I wanted to bring them up in England, you see?
I didn't want to move.
And today, the satisfaction I have is, thank God, they found very good partners.
And they're nice to me, too.
And they have their own families.
And I can see the way they behave, although sometimes they rebel, they're copied exactly when-- they're not in the way I brought them up to-- see, she just-- she works for Citizens Advice Bureau.
She helps them because she's a graduate from Manchester University--
In social studies.
The other one is a psychotherapist, analyst.
You know, they all help people, really, in their professions.
And the interesting part is that Irene's husband, who is an American, he took a PhD here in England, that's how she met him.
He's the one that writes on the Holocaust.
And he has written several books, and I have them here, yes.
Paul Marcus.
And he's still writing.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he does it from a psychological point of view.
And I asked him once, why did you want to do it.
I mean, you're so American, born in America, American parents.
He said when he heard after the war that one and a half million children had been murdered, he wanted to do something.
So he-- he is very busy with this.
And he, somehow, by reading all what he has read-- I sometimes tell him not to read so much.
It's not good for him.
But he read-- they have my tapes, but he only read the transcript.
And I think he understands me better now.
My children haven't heard the tapes, nor read the transcripts.
Irene listened to one.
She couldn't listen to more.
And I had a program on kaleidoscope, so that-- I only speak for 10 minutes.
So this, they listened to.
But they find it very hard, I find, in account.
And funnily enough, I find that the third daughter, Mickey, she's more interested, you know, to know what happened.
And she wants to know.
She probably will be the one that will take it on.
There's always one child that wants to take it on.
We have a problem, you see.
We have-- we find-- the children find it too difficult, because, you see, I have-- I live quite a lot in France.
And my family in France, they are the children of two survivors.
Janine has lived with the Holocaust from the minute she came out.
She has done an awful lot of good work in the Archives of [NON-ENGLISH], of who came back, who didn't come back.
She speaks in the schools, the Resistance.
She does an awful lot.
But I said I can't, years ago.
I wouldn't.
She used to be with me on holiday in France.
She used to ask me, she used to ask me questions.
She wanted to talk about it.
I said, leave me alone, I said.
And she said, you should speak to your children.
I said, what do you want from me.
Speak to my children?
They already have no father.
They wanted me to speak about the Holocaust?
How much can they take?
So I didn't want to talk.
So she said, only recently, in 1988, was I prepared to do the tapes for the library, British library, she said, at last, you're talking.
I said, I couldn't before.
It took me all these years.
The gathering in Israel in 1981, you could see from the 6,000 people that did come, that only then, for the first time, did people speak.
David, my brother, has never spoken.
He found, sitting at a terrace of the King David, he found somebody with a similar number as his tattoo.
And soon, they found out that they had done the same journey, through Gross-Rosen, Mauthausen internment camps.
And do you know that's the first time I found out his life story, whatever he spoke in this hour or two?
I didn't know anything about it.
I always had-- what terrible things.
Because you mustn't forget, I took him out of hospital with the bandages.
The first thing they did, they took the bandages off.
Can you see?
Then, apparently, he couldn't wake up, you see?
He couldn't see.
So they hit him, terribly.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He suffered terribly, both of them.
And the youngest, too, the youngest is a very, very sick man.
He has-- he's always-- his paranoiac.
You know, he-- he has always people are following him.
He was married with four children.
His marriage went to pieces because he's not-- I could see, she had a point.
He's not livable.
He's the best husband in the world.
And he is really a very nice father.
But he is now impossible because he has that-- he was here in London once, and, you know, he had a ticket to go to Paris.
And he took it to Strasbourg.
You see, he's always being followed.
The post doesn't come to his address.
He is afraid that someone is following him.
He is very, very sick.
He's very sick.
And he has been very ill.
And you can't do anything anymore for him except medication.
And he was the one who was terribly attached to my mother.
And that affected him terribly.
In the last few years, I have been trying to make it a better life.
I've been traveling a lot.
I spend a lot of time with my family in France.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I do some for the Holocaust.
I am now more involved with the schools.
You know, I-- I got involved a couple-- two years ago.
And then I was taken ill from it.
I had to stop it.
I got terrible headaches.
And I had to stop it for a year or so.
Now, slowly, I am getting into it again, but I have to be careful still.
And I took on, you know, some hobbies.
You know, I'm interested in theater.
I'm interested in music.
I'm interested in art, in photography, and so on and so forth.

So that's how I have been trying to make my life a little bit nicer, more enjoyable, and making up a little bit for the time when I was not free, because I wanted to see to the girls, and so on and so forth.
I hang about here and there with the children.
They don't ask too much of me.
But it gives me great satisfaction to see that they have good partners, lovely partners.
And they are so capable.
They're terribly capable, my daughters.
I say they shouldn't work so hard.
And they should take it easy.
But they don't know that.
Probably my fault.
But-- and I enjoy the grandchildren.
You know, that's really-- there, you see the continuation.
Every time we have a-- a Shema, we have a bar mitzvah, a bat mitzvah, it's-- emotionally, I get terribly involved because I can see the continuation.
Hitler wanted to finish us off as a race, as a people.
But here, I can see a continuation.
And where I didn't go in with my Auschwitz, because I still find it terribly painful, is all these children I saw arrive, you see.
And what I did with them.
And I didn't also go in with the testimonies I heard at the Barbie trial in Lyon, you know.
I wasn't a witness, but I was there.
And it was the first time he came up to be identified.
He had to be identified by some Resistance and by some deportees.
The terrible things they did.
Such horrors, you see, I can't even speak about it today, you know.
Because I know them, but I can't speak about it.
So but it doesn't mean that-- these things don't leave you.
You see, you don't even talk to anybody about it because people can't-- they can't and won't listen to it.
You know what hurts us, probably it's not their fault, the other party.
We have the impression that nobody can really understand us.
What we say, and what we see in front of us, how can they understand us?
We want to get through to the children, or through to the teachers, or through to people sometimes.
The question mark is can they follow us.
I even said, after a few days in the camp, when I saw what was happening, I said, if I come out of that hell, and I want to tell people what I see here, if they don't believe me, I will understand.
I said that right away.
I will understand.
That was a hell like there has never been a hell like that, and I hope there will never be a hell like that.
The message I would like to leave is [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I would like, first of all, that the revisionists shouldn't have their way.
We have today archives-- and archives from Russia, from Germany, from everywhere, which clearly show what Hitler had in mind.
And we have to bring that message forward.
And generations after generations, they should never forget.
Because if we do forget, it can happen again.
I think, as Primo Levi says, the good ones didn't come back.
Only the worst came back, he says.
I think it wasn't-- I mean, the fittest.
I think that everybody, not everybody-- I would hardly say-- I said some of us, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some luck, perhaps, some luck that we left at the-- I left Auschwitz at a certain time.
I could have died in Bergen-Belsen.
I didn't.
I left for Bergen-Belsen.
I went to a factory.
The very few days, it wasn't as bad, then it got worse, you know.
I kept-- I kept leaving camps.
But, in the meantime, hundreds died on the way every time.
I was meant to come out.
I sometimes say, why me.
You know, that's the question.
Why me?
I have a friend, she was in the same convoy as us from Saint-Etienne.
A lawyer, I don't want to mention her name.
I met her after the war in Strasbourg.

When she arrived at the camp, the most hopeless woman, an educated woman, a barrister, the most hopeless woman you can meet when it comes to ordinary things, we helped her out, you know, with what we could.
She hid in the so-called toilets there.
She couldn't work.
They'd kill-- they killed her off otherwise.
And I saw her after the war in Strasbourg.
They said she's here with her husband.
I said, never.
I met her in Strasbourg over a meal.
I said, Margaret, if you are here today, thousands of others should be here.
She says, how come.
I'm pleased to see you, but how come?
She said, I'll tell you the truth.
I was under review on the hospital.
She was selected for the gas chambers because she was already sent before.
And whoever was in charge of the review exchanged her for another person.
The other person probably was also on the list, but that's how she's here today.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And she was not a youngster.
She was-- another youngster.
Another youngster was over 30.
And she had a daughter.
And she-- her husband lived another good few years.
And her daughter is married with children, yes.
She has a good old age.
That's one of the cases that that's how she came out.
But she is a lovely person.
She-- that's how she made it otherwise.
And there was another case that I never thought she would come out, because she was already, when she was in the-- in the-- in the barracks, my mother couldn't keep-- she was always crying.
She is hungry.
So my mother gave her her own bread to keep her quiet.
And after the war, she came out.
I said to her, how did you make it.
How did you make it?
She came out.
She came out with-- that's the first time I saw her, just like this, absolutely little canals all along the arm where the lice were.
And yet, she made it.
And she found her daughter.
She was the only one who was taken.
She made it alive, a kind woman, but also, a hopeless woman.
And all the other ones who were through, trying to survive.
The ones, the beautiful girls from France who kept their hair and tried so hard to get easier jobs never got it.
So the message is really, that you mustn't forget the ones we left-- who died.
Also, the generations and the generations that we lost, you know.
By losing the six million, we lost the most wonderful people.
Generations and generations of wonderful people.
And I certainly will never forget them.
And I will never forgive them, the Germans either.
They say the Nazis, but the Germans were in it.
The Germans were in it, too.
All of them-- some of them.
So I personally say that we mustn't forget, just carry the message forward.
Well, I'm very pleased that Spielberg is making these recordings.
Because he will probably be more successful than anybody else, you know, by doing it all condensed the way he's doing, especially for teaching.
All the other-- all the other records we have done are in good hands, and in very good hands, in fact.
But I think that he probably, what you are doing will be perhaps used more than the others.
And that's what we did it for.
We want people to study, to know about it, and to carry it on, so that it shouldn't be forgotten.
And it should be a lesson, too.
